content.text: Opportunity Backlog Scoring and Prioritization

An enterprise Claude programme that relies on informal prioritisation — whoever advocates loudest, whoever has the most executive access, or whatever the CoE team found most interesting — will consistently deploy the wrong use cases first. A scoring model converts subjective enthusiasm into a comparable, defensible ranking that programme managers can explain to stakeholders and engineers can plan around.

This document defines a six-dimension scoring model for the Claude use case backlog, explains how to apply it, describes the four-tier classification system that organises the scored backlog into actionable queues, and provides the governance cadence that keeps the backlog current as the programme evolves.

attrs.alt: Six-dimension opportunity scoring model: business impact (25%), task volume (20%), Claude fit (20%), implementation effort (20%), data readiness (10%), strategic alignment (5%)

content.text: Why a Formal Scoring Model?
The case for a formal scoring model is not bureaucratic — it is practical. Without one, three predictable distortions emerge in every enterprise AI programme:
Recency bias:
The most recently workshopped idea gets the most development attention, even when earlier ideas in the backlog score higher on every objective dimension.
Political pressure:

Business units with louder voices or closer proximity to the executive sponsor get their use cases prioritised regardless of expected return. The CoE becomes a service bureau for the most influential stakeholders rather than a value maximiser.
Effort avoidance:

Without a model that explicitly weights business impact higher than implementation effort, teams naturally gravitate toward technically easy use cases — prompt-only solutions, internal tooling — and deprioritise the harder integrations that generate the most business value.

A formal scoring model does not eliminate judgment — it makes judgment auditable. When a business unit asks 'why is my use case not in the next sprint?', the answer is a score breakdown, not a political negotiation.

Score every item in the backlog, not just the ones you are about to deploy. An unscored item is an undecided item. Undecided items create conversations that are resolved by politics rather than analysis.
The Six Scoring Dimensions
D1: Business Impact (25%)

Business impact is the heaviest-weighted dimension because it is the primary justification for all programme investment. It scores the expected magnitude of Claude's effect on a business outcome that the organisation measures and cares about:
Score
Criteria
Example Use Cases
Directly affects revenue generation or major risk exposure (regulatory penalty, customer churn)
Proposal quality improvement in competitive sales, compliance document review
Significant efficiency gain — eliminates substantial human labour in a high-volume process
Customer support ticket summarisation at 10K tickets/month, invoice exception handling
Moderate improvement — meaningful time saving or quality lift in a mid-volume process
Meeting summary automation, job description drafting
Nice-to-have quality improvement — reduces friction but does not change productivity substantially
Internal FAQ chatbot for low-traffic policies, email template generation
Marginal or unclear business benefit — primarily a technology demonstration
Experimental chatbots with no defined use case, demos with no path to production
D2: Task Volume (20%)

Impact at scale requires volume. A perfectly fit use case that occurs twice a quarter does not generate enough return to justify integration investment. Volume scores use annual task count as the primary input:
Prefer system-derived counts (CRM activity logs, ticket system exports, ERP event counts) over survey-based estimates
When volume is seasonal, use annual total rather than peak monthly — seasonal programmes can still score high if the annual volume is sufficient
For new process steps that do not yet exist at scale (e.g. a proposed customer briefing process), estimate volume based on the number of customer interactions the team handles today
D3: Claude Fit (20%)

Claude fit scores how well the task's dominant activity type matches Claude's reliable capability profile. This dimension prevents investment in use cases where Claude's performance will be unacceptably variable:
Score 5:

Core language tasks — drafting, summarising, extracting, classifying text — with no external data retrieval required. Claude receives a document and produces an output. Minimal prompt engineering. Example: contract summary from pasted text.
Score 4:

Strong fit, with a simple API integration or retrieval step. Claude receives structured context from an upstream call and produces an output. Example: proposal section draft given a CRM opportunity JSON payload.
Score 3:

Moderate fit; requires retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), multi-step tool use, or significant prompt engineering to reach acceptable quality. Example: internal policy Q&A against a large, poorly-structured document corpus.
Score 2:

Partial fit; Claude handles one component but the use case also requires real-time data access, numerical computation, or multi-system orchestration that substantially increases complexity and failure surface.
Score 1:

Poor fit; the use case fundamentally requires capabilities Claude does not have — real-time market data, sub-second latency, deterministic rule execution, or high-precision numerical outputs where hallucination risk is unacceptable.
D4: Implementation Effort (20%, inverted)

Effort scores are inverted: a score of 5 means the use case can be deployed in days (prompt-only); a score of 1 means it requires months of engineering. This inversion is intentional — the dimension contributes positively to the overall score when effort is low.
Estimate effort using the following tier model:
Score
Effort Band
What This Typically Involves
Days (1–3)
A new prompt added to an existing Claude API wrapper; no new integrations; no UI changes
1–2 weeks
A new API endpoint, a simple pre-processing step (parse a document, call one upstream system), and a basic UI component
2–6 weeks
A RAG pipeline with document chunking and embedding, or multi-step tool use with two to three system integrations
6–12 weeks
Deep integration with a core enterprise system (ERP, CRM, HRIS) requiring SSO, audit logging, and change management
3+ months
Custom fine-tuning, a new model deployment, or a complex multi-agent orchestration with multiple fallback paths

Do not let 'estimated by the advocate' effort scores stand without review from a platform engineer. Advocates consistently underestimate effort by a factor of two to four. Require a 30-minute technical sanity check before finalising the D4 score for any Tier 1 candidate.
D5: Data Readiness (10%)

Data readiness scores whether the input data Claude needs is accessible, formatted appropriately, and free from regulatory barriers. Even a perfectly scoped use case fails if the data pipeline cannot be built within the deployment window:
5 — Data exists in a system the platform can access, is already in a text format Claude can process, is classified at an acceptable data sensitivity tier, and contains no PII or regulated fields
4 — Data exists and is accessible; minor preparation work (format conversion, anonymisation of one field, one new API permission) is needed
3 — Data exists but requires non-trivial preparation: data classification exercise, new data sharing agreement, schema documentation, or access controls review
2 — Data exists but has significant access barriers: legal hold, regulatory restriction, cross-jurisdictional data transfer issues, or requires consent workflows
1 — Data does not exist in digital form (paper-only), has never been digitised, or requires a new data collection project before Claude can be trained or prompted
D6: Strategic Alignment (5%)

Strategic alignment has the lowest weight because it is the most malleable dimension — a use case that scores well on the other five dimensions can often be framed to align with a strategic priority. The dimension exists to provide a tiebreaker between otherwise equal candidates and to surface cases where high-scoring use cases have no strategic home (which may indicate a gap in the organisation's OKR framework rather than a problem with the use case):
5 — The use case is explicitly named in a company OKR, a board-level initiative, or a published strategic plan
4 — The use case supports a divisional or function-level priority that is reviewed at the executive level quarterly
3 — The use case aligns to a function's stated goals for the year but is not explicitly tracked in a KPI
2 — The use case supports general efficiency goals but cannot be linked to a specific stated priority
1 — The use case has no clear connection to any organisational priority
Applying the Scoring Model
Score each use case in the backlog using the following structured process:

# Backlog scoring implementation
# Use this as the basis for a spreadsheet, Notion database, or lightweight app

def score_use_case(use_case):
    """
    Computes the composite opportunity score for a Claude use case.
    Each dimension must be scored 1–5 before calling this function.
    """
    weights = {
        "business_impact": 0.25,
        "task_volume": 0.20,
        "claude_fit": 0.20,
        "implementation_effort": 0.20,  # inverted: 5=easy, 1=hard
        "data_readiness": 0.10,
        "strategic_alignment": 0.05,
    }

    # Validate all dimensions are present and in range
    for dim, weight in weights.items():
        score = use_case.get(dim)
        if score is None:
            raise ValueError(f"Missing score for dimension: {dim}")
        if not (1 <= score <= 5):
            raise ValueError(f"Score for {dim} must be 1–5, got {score}")

    composite = sum(use_case[dim] * weight for dim in weights)

    # Determine tier
    if composite >= 4.0:
        tier = 1
        action = "Active sprint — pilot within 30 days"
    elif composite >= 3.0:
        tier = 2
        action = "Next-quarter queue — assign owner and feasibility review"
    elif composite >= 2.0:
        tier = 3
        action = "Watchlist — re-score quarterly"
    else:
        tier = 4
        action = "Archive — formal defer with documented rationale"

    return {
        "use_case": use_case["name"],
        "composite_score": round(composite, 2),
        "tier": tier,
        "action": action,
        "dimension_scores": {d: use_case[d] for d in weights},
    }

# Example use case
result = score_use_case({
    "name": "Pre-call customer briefing compiler",
    "business_impact": 4,      # significant time saving, high-volume sales team
    "task_volume": 4,           # ~1,200 customer calls/month
    "claude_fit": 4,            # strong fit; CRM pull + summarisation
    "implementation_effort": 3, # ~3 weeks; CRM integration + prompt tuning
    "data_readiness": 4,        # CRM accessible; minor field classification needed
    "strategic_alignment": 5,   # named in Q3 sales productivity OKR
})

# Output:
# {
#   "use_case": "Pre-call customer briefing compiler",
#   "composite_score": 3.9,
#   "tier": 2,
#   "action": "Next-quarter queue — assign owner and feasibility review",
#   "dimension_scores": {
#     "business_impact": 4, "task_volume": 4, "claude_fit": 4,
#     "implementation_effort": 3, "data_readiness": 4, "strategic_alignment": 5
#   }
# }
The Four-Tier Backlog System
Scored items are classified into four tiers that define the action taken on each item. The tier system transforms a ranked list into an actionable programme queue:

attrs.alt: Four-tier backlog system showing Tier 1 (active sprint), Tier 2 (next-quarter queue), Tier 3 (watchlist), and Archive with typical backlog sizes and governance actions

content.text: Managing Tier 1: Active Sprint

Tier 1 contains the use cases currently in active development or immediate pilot. Limit Tier 1 to three to five items at any time. Cramming more into Tier 1 than the team can run in parallel produces context-switching overhead that slows everything down.
Every Tier 1 item must have, before development begins:
A named owner (responsible for requirements, user acceptance, and adoption)
A completed feasibility assessment (signed off by the technical liaison)
A defined success metric with a baseline value captured
A pilot start date and a decision gate date (when the programme decides pilot → scale or stop)
Managing Tier 2: Next-Quarter Queue

Tier 2 is the programme's planning horizon — the use cases that will enter Tier 1 in the next one to three months as current pilots complete or scale. Tier 2 items should have a feasibility stub (the use case canvas from the ideation workshop) but do not need a full feasibility assessment yet.

Each Tier 2 item should have a documented reason it is not in Tier 1 — typically one of: capacity constraint (team is at the Tier 1 limit), data readiness gap (a prerequisite gap closure is in progress), or BU readiness (the business unit needs change management preparation before deployment).
Managing Tier 3: Watchlist

Tier 3 is the long backlog — use cases that are viable but not yet ready to prioritise. The primary value of Tier 3 is preventing good ideas from being lost and ensuring that the programme has a supply of well-defined candidates ready to promote when capacity opens.
Tier 3 items are re-scored quarterly. The most common reasons a Tier 3 item moves up to Tier 2:
A new Claude model release improves the Claude Fit score (e.g. longer context window enables a use case that was previously constrained by document length)
A data readiness gap closes (a data classification project completes, a new API becomes available, a legal barrier is resolved)
A business priority shift increases the Business Impact score (a new OKR is set that directly names the process the use case addresses)
Governance: The Quarterly Backlog Review
The scoring model produces a static ranking; the backlog review keeps it current. Every quarter, the programme manager and CoE lead run a 90-minute Quarterly Backlog Review with the following agenda:
New item intake (20 min):
Score all use cases that entered the backlog since the last review. Newly workshopped ideas, employee submissions, and vendor-suggested use cases. Calculate composite scores and assign to tiers.
Tier 1 retrospective (15 min):
For each completed Tier 1 pilot: did it pass the decision gate? Confirm scale or stop decisions. Remove closed items from the active sprint.
Tier 2 → Tier 1 promotion (20 min):
Review the top Tier 2 items. Confirm that blocking gaps are closed and readiness conditions are met. Promote items into Tier 1 up to the three-to-five-item limit.
Re-scoring (25 min):
Re-score Tier 3 items where context has changed: new model capabilities, closed data gaps, shifted business priorities. Promote Tier 3 items to Tier 2 if their score crosses 3.0.
Archive review (10 min):
Confirm that archived items remain appropriately deferred. Remove items that are no longer relevant (the underlying process has been eliminated, the pain has been addressed another way).
Score Calibration: Avoiding Common Scoring Errors
Even with a well-defined rubric, scoring errors creep in. Three calibration practices keep scores accurate and comparable across different assessors and business units:
Anchor Scoring

Before the programme's first backlog review, score two or three reference use cases collaboratively — one that should clearly be Tier 1, one that should clearly be Tier 3, and one borderline case. These become 'anchor items' that all future scorers reference when calibrating their own scores.

Publish the anchor items with their score breakdowns in the backlog tool. New programme team members score the anchors before scoring live items; if their anchor scores deviate by more than 0.5 points from the published scores, facilitate a calibration conversation before they score real backlog items.
Dual Scoring

For any use case that may enter Tier 1 in the next quarter, require two independent scorers — typically the proposing BU lead and the technical liaison — to score it separately before comparing. When their composite scores differ by more than 0.5 points, convene a 20-minute reconciliation session rather than averaging the scores. Averaging obscures the disagreement; reconciliation surfaces the assumption difference (usually about implementation effort or data readiness) and produces a better-informed final score.
Score Decay

Scores are not permanent. An implementation effort score of 5 (days to deploy) earned in Q1 may be outdated by Q3 if the team has shifted to a new platform architecture. A business impact score of 5 may decline if the business process has been partially automated by other means.

Apply a formal 'score decay review' every 12 months for items that have been in the backlog more than a year without being deployed. Stale scores produce prioritisation decisions based on assumptions that may no longer hold.
Communicating Backlog Decisions to Stakeholders
The backlog scoring model is most valuable when it is transparent to the stakeholders whose use cases are being prioritised. Two communication patterns work consistently:
The Score Explanation Email
When a BU lead asks why their use case is in Tier 2 rather than Tier 1, send a structured response rather than a verbal explanation:

Subject: Backlog Score Update — [Use Case Name]

Hi [Name],

Here's the current scoring for "[Use Case Name]" from the Q3 backlog review:

| Dimension              | Score | Weight | Weighted |
|------------------------|-------|--------|----------|
| Business Impact        |   4   |  25%   |   1.00   |
| Task Volume            |   3   |  20%   |   0.60   |
| Claude Fit             |   4   |  20%   |   0.80   |
| Implementation Effort  |   3   |  20%   |   0.60   |
| Data Readiness         |   2   |  10%   |   0.20   |
| Strategic Alignment    |   4   |   5%   |   0.20   |
| **Composite**          |       |        | **3.40** |

**Current Tier: 2** (Next-Quarter Queue)

**Why it's not in Tier 1 right now:**
The primary constraint is Data Readiness (score: 2). The customer interaction data
that Claude needs to compile briefings is in the legacy CRM and requires a new data
sharing agreement with IT security before we can pipe it to the API.

**What would move it to Tier 1:**
When the data access request (submitted 2026-06-15, expected approval 2026-08-01)
is approved, we'll re-score Data Readiness to 4. That would bring the composite
to 3.6, which is still Tier 2, but with the Q3 sprint slots opening up in September
as two current pilots complete, we expect to promote this in the Q3 review.

Let me know if you'd like to discuss.

This format turns a potential grievance into a collaborative conversation. The BU lead sees exactly what is driving the tier placement and what they can do to improve it (in this case, accelerating the data access approval).
The Backlog Transparency Dashboard

For programmes with more than 20 active backlog items, maintain a shared read-only dashboard that any employee can view. The dashboard should show: each use case name, its tier, its composite score, the top blocking factor for Tier 2/3 items, and the last review date. No score breakdown is necessary on the dashboard — the summary is sufficient for self-service visibility.
Transparency dashboards reduce ad-hoc stakeholder inquiries by 60–70% in programmes that implement them. Most questions are answered before they are asked.
Integrating the Backlog with the Programme Roadmap

The scored backlog and the programme roadmap are different artefacts that must be synchronised. The backlog is the supply of potential work; the roadmap is the committed plan of what gets built and when. Four synchronisation rules maintain coherence between them:
Rule 1 — Roadmap slots are reserved, not specified:

When building a quarterly roadmap, reserve deployment slots (e.g. 'Q3 Slot 1: three-week pilot deployment') rather than specifying which use case fills each slot. Assign use cases to slots at the start of the quarter based on current scores — not three months in advance when scores may have changed.
Rule 2 — Score changes trigger roadmap review:

If a Tier 1 item's score drops below 4.0 mid-quarter (the data access is denied, the BU owner leaves, the business priority shifts), the roadmap must be revisited. Do not continue deploying a use case that has fallen below the prioritisation threshold.
Rule 3 — Roadmap capacity informs Tier 1 limit:

The number of items in Tier 1 should match the team's actual deployment capacity. If the platform team can run two pilots in parallel, Tier 1 should have exactly two items. Tier 1 is not a wish list — it is a commitment.
Rule 4 — Backlog is reviewed before roadmap is committed:
The Quarterly Backlog Review happens before the quarterly roadmap commitment, not after. Roadmaps built before backlog review commit to items that may not be the current highest-priority options.
Summary
Opportunity backlog scoring
is the discipline that converts a list of ideas into a defensible, comparable, sequenced programme queue.
Score every backlog item on six dimensions: business impact (25%), task volume (20%), Claude fit (20%), implementation effort (20% inverted), data readiness (10%), and strategic alignment (5%)
Composite scores above 4.0 enter the active sprint; 3.0–3.9 queue for next quarter; 2.0–2.9 go to the watchlist; below 2.0 are formally archived
Limit Tier 1 to three to five items at any time — more creates context-switching overhead that slows all deployments
Require a technical sanity check on implementation effort scores before any item enters Tier 1
Run a 90-minute Quarterly Backlog Review to intake new items, re-score changed items, and manage tier promotions
The scoring model makes prioritisation auditable — 'why is my use case not in the sprint?' has a score-based answer, not a political one

Programmes that implement this scoring model consistently deploy two to three times as many use cases per quarter as those that prioritise informally — not because they work faster, but because they work on the right things first.
